title: Screen time and early adolescent mental health, academic, and social outcomes in 9- and 10- year old children: Utilizing the Adolescent Brain Cognitive Development â  (ABCD) Study.
abstract: In a technology-driven society, screens are being used more than ever. The high rate of electronic media use among children and adolescents begs the question: is screen time harming our youth? The current study draws from a nationwide sample of 11,875 participants in the United States, aged 9 to 10 years, from the Adolescent Brain Cognitive Development Study (ABCD StudyÂ®). We investigate relationships between screen time and mental health, behavioral problems, academic performance, sleep habits, and peer relationships by conducting a series of correlation and regression analyses, controlling for SES and race/ethnicity. We find that more screen time is moderately associated with worse mental health, increased behavioral problems, decreased academic performance, and poorer sleep, but heightened quality of peer relationships. However, effect sizes associated with screen time and the various outcomes were modest; SES was more strongly associated with each outcome measure. Our analyses do not establish causality and the small effect sizes observed suggest that increased screen time is unlikely to be directly harmful to 9-and-10-year-old children.

FT_IC1.evidence: Uses ABCD Study. Methods: 'All data were from the existing Adolescent Brain Cognitive Development (ABCD) dataset... which draws from 21 research sites across the country, which in total recruited 11,875 children, ages 9 to 10 years.' Analysis on sample of 11,727 children ages 9 to 10.92 years.

FT_IC2.evidence: Measures specific screen time modalities. Methods: 'A 14-question Screen Time Questionnaire (STQ) was completed by the children, providing self-report measures of screen time use, divided by weekdays and weekends... television shows and movies, videos, video games, texting, social media, and video chat.' Results examine relationships between different types of screen time and outcomes.

FT_IC3.evidence: Correlation and regression analyses linking screen time to mental health, academic, and social outcomes. Methods: 'Bivariate Pearson correlations between each of the variables were computed... Multiple linear regressions were then run, separately by sex.' Results reported in Tables 1-4 with statistical tests (t-statistics, p-values).

FT_IC4.evidence: Reports t-statistics, standardized betas, partial correlations, and p-values. Table 2: Main effect of screen time on depression (Standardized Beta=0.018, t=1.24, p=.214); on externalizing (Standardized Beta=0.083, t=5.69, p<.001*); on ADHD (Standardized Beta=0.088, t=6.04, p<.001*).

FT_IC5.evidence: Original empirical analysis. Study design: cross-sectional baseline ABCD analysis examining correlations and regressions between screen time and various outcomes including depression, anxiety, academic performance, sleep, and peer relationships.

FT_IC6.evidence: Journal article published in PLoS ONE, 2021;16(9):e0256591. Not a preprint, conference abstract, or duplicate.

C1.FT_EC7_reason: Cross-sectional study measuring screen time and outcomes at the same timepoint. FT-EC7 criteria (b) and (c) require longitudinal/prospective design with demographic-only predictors of screen time as DV, which does not apply here. Additionally, paper includes psychological/clinical predictors and reports screen time→outcome associations.

FT_IC1.evidence: Uses U.S. ABCD Study, N=11,875 participants, ages 9-10 years, nationwide sample

FT_IC2.evidence: Primary focus is on generic 'total screen time' composite aggregating multiple modalities (TV, videos, video games, texting, social media, video chat). Although paper examines specific modalities in Table 1, the primary exposure variable reported across most analyses is 'Total Screen Time,' an aggregate measure. Per FT-IC2 guidelines, mixed-modality papers with only aggregate composite reported = NOT_MET.

FT_IC3.evidence: Correlation and regression analyses conducted. Table 2 shows multiple linear regressions with screen time as predictor and various outcomes (depression, anxiety, academic performance, etc.)

FT_IC4.evidence: Table 2 reports standardized betas and p-values (e.g., Main effect of ST on Depression: β=0.018, p=.214)

FT_IC5.evidence: Original empirical analysis: cross-sectional and correlational analyses from ABCD baseline data

FT_IC6.evidence: Published journal article, PLoS One, 2021, DOI: 10.1371/journal.pone.0256591

C2.FT_EC7_reason: Screen time is the independent variable in analyses; mental health, academic, and social outcomes are dependent variables (non-SM outcomes). Cross-sectional design. EC7 does not apply.

title: 24-Hour Movement Behaviors and Impulsivity.
abstract: BACKGROUND: The objective of this study was to examine individual and concurrent associations between meeting the Canadian 24-Hour Movement Guidelines for Children and Youth (9-11 hours of sleep per night, â¤2 hours of recreational screen time (ST) per day, and at least 60 minutes of moderate to vigorous physical activity per day) and dimensions of impulsivity. METHODS: Data from this cross-sectional observational study were part of the first annual curated release of the Adolescent Brain Cognitive Development Study. Participants included 4524 children between the ages of 8 and 11 years. RESULTS: In analyses, it was shown that adherence to individual movement behavior recommendations as well as combinations of adherence to movement behavior recommendations were associated with each dimension of impulsivity. Meeting all 3 movement behavior recommendations was associated with lower positive urgency (95% confidence interval [CI]: -0.12 to -0.05), negative urgency (95% CI: -0.04 to -0.08), Behavioral Inhibition System (95% CI: -0.08 to -0.01), greater perseverance (95% CI: 0.09 to 0.15), and better scores on delay-discounting (95% CI: 0.57 to 0.94). Meeting the ST and sleep recommendations was associated with less impulsive behaviors on all dimensions of impulsivity: negative urgency (95% CI: -0.20 to -0.10), positive urgency (95% CI: -0.16 to -0.08), perseverance (95% CI: 0.06 to 0.15), Behavioral Inhibition System (95% CI: -0.15 to -0.03), Behavioral Activation System (BAS) reward responsiveness (95% CI: -0.04 to -0.05), BAS drive (95% CI: -0.14 to -0.06), BAS fun-seeking (95% CI: -0.15 to -0.17), and delay-discounting task (95% CI: 0.68 to 0.97). CONCLUSIONS: Findings support efforts to determine if limiting recreational ST while promoting adequate sleep enhances the treatment and prevention of impulsivity-related disorders.

FT_IC1.evidence: Uses ABCD Study: 'first annual curated release of the Adolescent Brain Cognitive Development Study. Participants included 4524 children between the ages of 8 and 11 years'

FT_IC2.evidence: Measures recreational screen time (ST) as SM/phone variable: 'Canadian 24-Hour Movement Guidelines for Children and Youth...≤2 hours of recreational screen time (ST) per day'

FT_IC3.evidence: Reports inferential statistics linking ST recommendations to impulsivity outcomes: 'Meeting the ST and sleep recommendations was associated with less impulsive behaviors on all dimensions' with CIs and p-values reported

FT_IC4.evidence: Reports extractable statistics: 95% confidence intervals and coefficients throughout results (e.g., 'negative urgency (95% CI: −0.20 to −0.10)'; Table 3 contains standardized β coefficients and p-values)

FT_IC5.evidence: Original empirical analysis: cross-sectional observational study examining associations between movement behavior recommendations and impulsivity dimensions

C1.FT_EC7_reason: ST is modeled as part of movement behavior recommendations (DV context), but in this cross-sectional analysis, ST itself is treated as an independent variable (meeting vs not meeting ST recommendation). However, the primary direction appears to be movement behavior combinations predicting impulsivity, not the reverse. The paper is fundamentally cross-sectional, which negates FT-EC7 application per E7 binding rule.

FT_IC1.evidence: Paper explicitly states 'Data from this study were part of the first annual curated release of the Adolescent Brain Cognitive Development Study. Participants included 4524 children between the ages of 8 and 11 years.' ABCD is the U.S. ABCD Study.

FT_IC2.evidence: Screen time (ST) measured as recreational screen time via Youth Screen Time Survey (12 items), encompassing watching shows and movies, watching videos, video games, texting, and other digital activities on weekday and weekend days.

FT_IC3.evidence: Multiple inferential tests reported linking ST and movement behavior combinations to impulsivity dimensions. Example: Meeting ST + sleep recommendations was negatively associated with positive urgency (95% CI: -0.20 to -0.10).

FT_IC4.evidence: Numeric coefficients reported throughout. Example: β = -0.12 (p<0.001) for ST + sleep recommendations vs positive urgency; β = -0.09 (95% CI: -0.12 to -0.05) for ST + sleep vs positive urgency.

FT_IC5.evidence: Cross-sectional empirical analysis of ABCD baseline data examining associations between 24-hour movement behaviors (including ST) and impulsivity dimensions using structural equation modeling.

FT_IC6.evidence: Peer-reviewed journal article published in Pediatrics, 2019, with distinct analyses from other ABCD studies.

title: Sociodemographic Associations With Early Smartphone Ownership in US Adolescents
abstract: â¦ This cutoff was based on prior studies showing youth receive smartphones near the age of 10 years on average.Analyses applied Adolescent Brain Cognitive Development (ABCD) â¦

FT_IC1.evidence: U.S. ABCD Study. Methods: 'The ongoing ABCD Study collected demographic and screen use data (among other domains) from 11 878 children and primary caregivers at baseline (mean age = 9.9 years) beginning in 2016 with annual follow-up assessments. The analytic sample included all participants with complete data (N = 9475).'

FT_IC2.evidence: Smartphone ownership age as specific exposure variable. Methods: 'Parents were asked each year at what age their child received their first smartphone.' Outcome coded as 'Early smartphone ownership (<10 years old)' modeled as binary outcome.

FT_IC3.evidence: Cross-sectional multivariable logistic regression examining associations between sociodemographic predictors and smartphone ownership age. Methods: 'We then estimated a cross-sectional multivariable logistic regression model to examine associations between key sociodemographic predictors (sex, race/ethnicity, household income, household structure, and parental education) and first smartphone ownership age.'

FT_IC4.evidence: Odds ratios (ORs) and 95% confidence intervals reported in Table 2. Example: Female OR = 1.38 (95% CI 1.19-1.60); Black OR = 2.78 (95% CI 2.07-3.64); Single parent OR = 2.01 (95% CI 1.67-2.44); all p < .05 or p < .001 noted.

FT_IC5.evidence: Original empirical ABCD analysis. Methods describe cross-sectional multivariable logistic regression analyses of baseline data with complete demographic and smartphone ownership information.

FT_IC6.evidence: Published journal article in Pediatrics, Volume 157, Issue 2, February 2026, e2025073891. Not a preprint or duplicate.

C1.FT_EC7_reason: All three conditions met: (a) Smartphone ownership age is DEPENDENT variable in all reported analyses; (b) ALL predictors are demographic/sociodemographic only (sex, race/ethnicity, household income, household structure, parental education); (c) Cross-sectional analysis only. No individual psychological/clinical/behavioral/neural outcomes examined. FT_EC7 applies per binding rule E14 (sociodemographic predictors of phone/SM, cross-sectional).

FT_IC1.evidence: Uses U.S. ABCD Study data: 'The ongoing ABCD Study collected demographic and screen use data...from 11 878 children and primary caregivers at baseline (mean age = 9.9 years) beginning in 2016' (N=9475 in analytic sample)

FT_IC2.evidence: Specific smartphone variable: age of first smartphone ownership, operationalized as binary (<10 years vs ≥10 years) and continuous (mean 10.9 years, SD 1.5). Measures smartphone ownership explicitly.

FT_IC3.evidence: Cross-sectional multivariable logistic regression examining associations between sociodemographic predictors (sex, race/ethnicity, household income, household structure, parental education) and early smartphone ownership as binary outcome

FT_IC4.evidence: Multiple odds ratios reported with 95% CIs: Female OR=1.38 (95% CI 1.19–1.60); Black OR=2.78 (95% CI 2.07–3.64); Single parent OR=2.01 (95% CI 1.67–2.44); <Bachelor's degree OR=1.70 (95% CI 1.40–2.06); all p<.001 or p<.01

FT_IC5.evidence: Original empirical study: cross-sectional logistic regression analysis from ABCD baseline data with inferential statistics and hypothesis testing

FT_IC6.evidence: Journal article, Pediatrics, February 2026, published online November 2025

C2.FT_EC7_reason: Paper is cross-sectional (not longitudinal). FT-EC7 only applies to longitudinal analyses predicting future smartphone use from baseline demographics. Per E7: cross-sectional analyses with smartphone/SM as DV do not trigger FT-EC7 exclusion.
